verses.text: Bildad bluntly demands how long Job will speak so, insisting God never perverts justice — and that Job's children must have died for their own sin. He defends God by condemning the dead. Zeal for God's justice, untempered by love, can speak with brutal cruelty.

Bildad bluntly demands how long Job will speak so, insisting God never perverts justice — and that Job's children must have died for their own sin. He defends God by condemning the dead. Zeal for God's justice, untempered by love, can speak with brutal cruelty.

If Job will seek God and be pure, Bildad says, God will surely restore and even multiply his end. He offers prosperity as the reward of repentance. The promise sounds pious, but it rests on the false notion that suffering always means hidden sin.

He appeals to the wisdom of the ancients, urging Job to learn from the fathers of old. He leans on inherited tradition. There is value in the wisdom of ages, yet tradition alone cannot fathom the deeper purposes of God.

He appeals to the wisdom of the ancients, urging Job to learn from the fathers of old. He leans on inherited tradition. There is value in the wisdom of ages, yet tradition alone cannot fathom the deeper purposes of God.

Can a reed grow without water? So, says Bildad, are the paths of all who forget God, whose hope shall perish. He likens the godless to a plant soon withered. The image is apt for hypocrites, but he wrongly aims it at a faithful man.

The hypocrite's confidence, he says, is a spider's web; he leans on his house, and it will not stand. He pictures false hope collapsing. A trust built on anything but God is indeed as frail as gossamer.

The hypocrite's confidence, he says, is a spider's web; he leans on his house, and it will not stand. He pictures false hope collapsing. A trust built on anything but God is indeed as frail as gossamer.

Though such a man seems green and well-rooted among the stones, he is plucked up, and others spring up in his place. His flourishing is brief. The prosperity of the godless has no lasting root, however green it looks for a season.

Though such a man seems green and well-rooted among the stones, he is plucked up, and others spring up in his place. His flourishing is brief. The prosperity of the godless has no lasting root, however green it looks for a season.

Bildad ends that God will not cast away a perfect man nor help the evildoer, and will yet fill Job's mouth with laughing. His conclusion holds a real comfort. God does not abandon the upright, though the way to that joy is not the simple path Bildad imagines.
